▪ Ahmedbhoy Habbibhoy v. Bombay Fire & Marine Insurance Co (1912) After occupation of the premises by the insurer, the insured’s machinery suffered through lack of maintenance, and it was held that the insurer was liable for the resultant depreciation.
paragraphs.textJP:
保険者は、権利の行使方法について注意を払わなければなりません。保険者は合理的に行動しなければならず、そうでなければ権利を失う可能性があります。以下の判例がこれを示しています。
▪ Cumberland v. Albert Insurance Co（1866年）：保険者が被保険者に通知せずに自動車のサルベージを売却した場合、保険者は代位の権利を喪失した。

paragraphs.text: Claim disputes between the insured and insurers may arise, because the insured is dissatisfied with the insurer with respect to the quantum of loss, or if the insurer denies liability for the loss. Although court proceedings may be instituted to settle such disputes, they can be expensive and time consuming. Hence, other means of settlement, such as mediation, arbitration and having the Financial Industry Disputes Resolution Centre Ltd (FIDReC) involved in settling such disputes are considered, instead of seeking a legal resolution through court proceedings which invariably involves onerous court procedures and legal expenses.
paragraphs.textJP: 被保険者と保険者の間の保険金請求の紛争は、被保険者が損失の金額に関して保険者に不満を持つ場合、または保険者が損失に対する賠償責任を否認する場合に生じることがあります。紛争解決のために裁判手続きが開始される場合がありますが、代替的な紛争解決手段も利用可能です。

paragraphs.text: We shall examine these three basic types of claim dispute settlements. Basic Insurance Concepts & Principles
paragraphs.textJP: これら3つの基本的な保険金請求の紛争解決方法について検討しましょう。

paragraphs.text: Mediation is a common and effective form of alternative dispute resolution in which the parties agree to submit any dispute to a neutral mediator, whose purpose is to achieve a mutually acceptable settlement and compromise of the dispute, rather than issue a formal ruling and decision on the merits, as occurs in arbitration. Depending upon the parties' agreement, the results of mediation can be binding, or non-binding. It is a voluntary process in which an impartial third party, namely a mediator, assists parties involved in a dispute to resolve their differences in an amicable manner. Lawyers may represent the parties during the process. The mediator’s opinion is strictly on a without-prejudice basis. More details on mediation are available from the website of Singapore Mediation Centre at: www.mediation.com.sg
paragraphs.textJP: 調停は、当事者が紛争を中立的な調停人に付託することに合意する、一般的で効果的な代替的紛争解決手段です。調停人の目的は、裁定を下すのではなく、紛争の相互に受け入れ可能な和解と妥協を達成することです。

paragraphs.text: If the dispute concerns only the amount of compensation (called “quantum”), arbitration may be resorted to as a means of resolving the dispute. If the dispute is regarding the denial of claims by insurers, FIDReC may well be involved.
paragraphs.textJP: 紛争が補償額（「quantum」と呼ばれます）のみに関するものである場合、紛争解決の手段として仲裁に訴えることがあります。紛争が保険者による保険金請求の否認に関するものである場合、FIDReCが関与することがあります。

paragraphs.text: Insurance contracts have provisions in them for parties to refer to arbitration in the event of “all disputes”, or the clause may specify that the dispute must be one in respect of “quantum”.
paragraphs.textJP: 保険契約には、「すべての紛争」の場合に仲裁に付託するための規定が含まれているか、または紛争が「quantum」に関するものでなければならないと規定する場合があります。

paragraphs.text: More details on arbitration are available from the website of Singapore International Arbitration Centre at: www.siac.org.sg
paragraphs.textJP: 仲裁の詳細は、Singapore International Arbitration Centreのウェブサイト（www.siac.org.sg）で入手できます。

paragraphs.text: Despite attempts at negotiation and alternative forms of dispute resolution, such as mediation and arbitration, there will be occasions when a claims handler is unable to negotiate an amicable solution to a claim. In these circumstances, a claimant may have to resort to litigation.
paragraphs.textJP: 調停や仲裁などの代替的紛争解決手段による交渉の試みにもかかわらず、保険金請求担当者が保険金請求の友好的な解決策を交渉できない場合があります。このような場合、請求者は裁判所の訴訟手続きを通じて保険金請求を追求することを選択できます。

paragraphs.text: Litigation is the word used to describe the use of the courts by parties in a dispute to hear, and to obtain a ruling on their differences of opinion.
paragraphs.textJP: 訴訟とは、紛争当事者が裁判所を利用して意見の相違について審理を受け、裁定を得ることを表す言葉です。

subsections.label: D
subsections.title: General Differences Between Mediation & Arbitration
subsections.titleJP: 調停と仲裁の一般的な違い

paragraphs.text: In a mediation process, the mediator helps the parties to settle their disputes by discussing and narrowing differences. The mediator helps the parties to arrive at an agreed and negotiated solution. He does not decide on the dispute. A successful mediation results in an agreement signed by the parties. An arbitration process involves both parties setting out to an arbitrator and results in a binding decision by the arbitrator himself, without the agreement of the parties concerned. In mediation, there is no such thing as a winning or losing party, because there is no binding decision, without both parties agreeing to one.
paragraphs.textJP: 調停のプロセスでは、調停人は当事者が紛争を議論し、意見の相違を狭めることで解決するのを支援します。調停人は当事者が合意に基づく交渉による解決策に到達するのを助けます。調停人は紛争について決定を下しません。調停が成功した場合、和解合意書が作成されます。

paragraphs.text: In an arbitration process, the arbitrator looks into the legal rights and wrongs of a dispute and makes a decision. Once the arbitrator has arrived at a decision, it is usually binding on the parties whether or not they may agree with it. It is very much like the way a court case is decided by a judge, except that the process does not take place in a court room, and it is not open to the public. As in a court case, there is usually a winning and a losing party in an arbitration process.
paragraphs.textJP: 仲裁のプロセスでは、仲裁人は紛争の法的な正否を検討し、裁定を下します。仲裁人が裁定に達すると、当事者がそれに同意するか否かにかかわらず、通常は拘束力を持ちます。仲裁裁定は裁判所に強制的に執行されることができます。

paragraphs.text: Arbitration is a less formal procedure than court litigation, and it is conducted in private, away from the glare of the media and the public. Parties are free to appoint their own arbitrators and can choose more practical procedures and rules for the conduct of arbitration. Generally, arbitration can also be more costefficient and speedier than court litigation in which the proceedings involved can be expensive and time consuming.
paragraphs.textJP: 仲裁は裁判訴訟よりも非公式な手続きであり、メディアや一般市民の注目から離れた私的な場で行われます。当事者は自らの仲裁人を選任する自由があり、紛争解決のためのより実用的な手続きと規則を選択することができます。

paragraphs.text: A typical Mediation/Arbitration clause appearing as a condition in a policy reads as follows: “All disputes arising out of this Policy may be submitted to the Singapore Mediation Centre for settlement by mediation in accordance with the mediation procedure for the time being in force, if the parties so agree. The parties agree to take part in the mediation in good faith and undertake to honour the terms of any settlement reached. If any dispute is not referred to mediation or if mediation fails, the dispute has to be referred to arbitration. Arbitration shall be conducted in accordance with the Arbitration Rules of the Singapore International Arbitration Centre.” Basic Insurance Concepts & Principles Appendix 7A ABC INSURANCE COMPANY (SINGAPORE) LTD (Registration No: 2009-12345-A) 21 Any Street, ABC Centre, Singapore 654321 Tel: (65) 6789 8181 Fax: (65) 6789 8282 E-mail: contactus@abcinco.com.sg Website: www.abcinco.com.sg PROPERTY/LIABILITY CLAIM FORM IMPORTANT: This form must be completed and returned to us AS SOON AS POSSIBLE. No liability is admitted by us by the issue of this form. NOTE: Sections I & II must be completed. (I) PERSONAL PARTICULARS Name of Insured NRIC No. (For Individuals) Address Postal Code Tel No. Fax Policy Type Policy No. Business / Occupation For company/business/organisations - Are you GST-registered? E-mail Address (II) DETAILS OF OCCURRENCE Date & Time Place Please give an account of how the incident occurred. Please give particulars of person responsible for the loss/damage/injury. Have you made a claim upon the person responsible for the loss/damage/injury? If claim for damage/loss is arising from theft/malicious act, please give us details of where and when the police report was made. How was entry into premises gained? Was there any signs or evidence of forcible and violent entry? Was the premises occupied at the time of the occurrence? If not, when was it last occupied? Particulars of eyewitnesses, if any. Please give us particulars of persons other than yourself who have any interest in the property concerned and state the nature of their interest. If there are other insurance covering the property concerned, please state the names of the insurers and their policy numbers. Please state the current total value of all the property insured under the policy. Please give details of your claim in Section (IV). SPECIMEN (III) LIABILITY CLAIM This section should be filled only if a claim is being made against you. When were you first notified of the incident? If loss/damage/injury is attributed to defects in your Premises, equipment or plant, please give us details. If anyone has been injured, please give: (a) full particulars of person injured (b) details of injuries sustained Has any intimation of claim been made against you? If so, by whom? (Please give details of the claim amount in Section IV) NOTE: No payment, offer or promise of any payment or admission of any liability should be made. All letters from third parties should be forwarded to us immediately upon receipt. (IV) DETAILS OF CLAIM Please give details of your claim below: Description of Item Details of damage/loss Date Purchased/Date Incurred Cost Amount Claimed NOTE: Relevant invoices and other documents to support your claim should accompany this form. I/We hereby declare that the statements and particulars given by me/us in this form are true and correct. Signature of Insured & Company stamp Date SPECIMEN
paragraphs.textJP: 保険証券の条件として記載される典型的な調停／仲裁条項は以下のように読みます。「本保険証券から生じるすべての紛争は、シンガポール調停センター（Singapore Mediation Centre）の調停手続きに基づく調停による解決に付託することができます。」

chapters.title: REINSURANCE AND CO-INSURANCE
outline: Settlement Of Claims Under Co-Insurance Policies
outlineJP: 共同保険における保険金請求の決済
再保険と共同保険の比較
learningOutcomes: define reinsurance and describe the cedant and the reinsurer
understand the reinsurance transaction process in general
understand the objectives of reinsurance
discuss the various methods of reinsurance: - facultative reinsurance - treaty reinsurance
explain co-insurance
understand how claims are settled under co-insurance
explain the differences between co-insurance and reinsurance
learningOutcomesJP: 再保険の概念を理解する
再保険の目的を知る
再保険の方法（任意再保険と特約再保険）を知る
比例再保険と非比例再保険の違いを理解する
共同保険の概念を理解する
共同保険における保険金請求の決済方法を知る
再保険と共同保険の違いを知る

paragraphs.text: Reinsurance is an extension of the fundamental concept of insurance, namely the sharing of risks. It is a risk transfer mechanism, whereby the original insurer (called the reinsured, cedant or ceding company) cedes or transfers part of a single risk or several risks to another insurer or insurers. The company that accepts the transfer of risks is known as the reinsurer.
paragraphs.textJP: 再保険は、リスクの分担という保険の基本概念の延長です。再保険はリスク移転メカニズムであり、元の保険者（再保険付保者、出再者、出再会社と呼ばれます）が単一のリスクまたは複数のリスクの一部を別の保険者に出再または移転します。リスクの移転を引き受ける会社は再保険者として知られています。

paragraphs.text: As we have discussed earlier in this study guide, insurance is “a risk transfer mechanism”. As it can be seen from above, reinsurance is also a risk transfer mechanism. This is because reinsurance is purchased by insurers to protect them against certain types of claims. Reinsurance can be defined as “insurance of risks assumed by an insurer”, or simply put as “insurance of insurance”. It is best thought of as “insurance for insurance companies”, a way for an insurer to protect against catastrophic or extraordinary losses, so for the purposes of this study text we shall define reinsurance as: Reinsurance: “Insurance for insurance companies”.
paragraphs.textJP:
本学習ガイドの前の部分で議論したように、保険は「リスク移転メカニズム」です。上記から分かるように、再保険もまたリスク移転メカニズムです。これは、再保険が一定の種類の保険金請求から保険者を保護するために保険者によって購入されるためです。再保険は「保険者が引き受けたリスクの保険」、または簡単に言えば「保険の保険」と定義できます。「保険会社のための保険」と考えるのが最も適切です。保険者が巨大災害的または異常な損失から身を守る方法です。
再保険：「保険会社のための保険」

paragraphs.text: Let us now consider the reinsurance transaction process in following Diagram 8.1: Diagram 8.1: Reinsurance Transaction Process
paragraphs.textJP:
ここで、以下のDiagram 8.1の再保険取引プロセスについて考えましょう。
Diagram 8.1：再保険取引プロセス

paragraphs.text: Note: Intermediaries (reinsurance brokers) are usually but not always involved in the process.
paragraphs.textJP: 注：仲介者（再保険ブローカー）は通常関与しますが、常にそうとは限りません。

paragraphs.text: A contract of reinsurance is strictly between the direct insurer and the reinsurer. The original insured is not a party to any reinsurance agreement that the direct insurer enters into. The direct insurer remains liable to the original insured for the whole risk that is accepted. If the insurer has purchased reinsurance, then the insurer will seek recovery from the reinsurer separately. When an insurer purchases reinsurance we refer to the insurer as the “ceding insurer” because the insurer is passing some of the risk to one or more reinsurers; we might say that the insurer is ceding some of the risk to one or more reinsurers.
paragraphs.textJP: 再保険契約は、厳密には元受保険者と再保険者の間の契約です。元の被保険者は、元受保険者が締結する再保険契約の当事者ではありません。元受保険者は、引き受けたリスク全体について元の被保険者に対して責任を負い続けます。保険者が再保険を購入した場合、保険者は再保険者に対して別途回収を求めます。保険者が再保険を購入する場合、保険者を「出再保険者」と呼びます。保険者がリスクの一部を1つ以上の再保険者に移転しているためです。

paragraphs.text: There is no communication or any contractual relationship between the original insured and the reinsurer.
paragraphs.textJP: 元の被保険者と再保険者の間にはコミュニケーションも契約関係もありません。

paragraphs.text: It is also common for reinsurers themselves to transfer risks. In other words, reinsurers also purchase reinsurance. When this happens, the act of the reinsurer passing risks to other reinsurers is called a retrocession. The assuming reinsurer is the retrocessionaire, and the ceding reinsurer is the retrocedent. Diagram 8.2: Retrocession Transaction Process
paragraphs.textJP:
再保険者自身がリスクを移転することも一般的です。言い換えれば、再保険者もまた再保険を購入します。これが行われる場合、再保険者が他の再保険者にリスクを移転する行為は再々保険（レトロセッション）と呼ばれます。リスクを引き受ける再保険者は再々保険者（レトロセッショネア）であり、出再する再保険者は再出再者（レトロセデント）です。
Diagram 8.2：再々保険取引プロセス

paragraphs.text: Think about why insurers might want to buy reinsurance. To help you answer this question, think about the reasons you might buy insurance to protect your own possessions. If you owned a business, why would you want to buy insurance?
paragraphs.textJP: 保険者がなぜ再保険を購入したいと思うか考えてみましょう。この質問に答えるために、自分の所有物を保護するために保険を購入する理由について考えてみてください。事業を所有していた場合、なぜ保険を購入したいと思いますか？

paragraphs.text: If you were sitting on the board of an insurance company then you would be asking yourself the same questions about buying insurance. But, as we have already seen, when an insurer buys insurance this is called “reinsurance”. So, why do insurers buy reinsurance?
paragraphs.textJP: 保険会社の取締役会にいるのであれば、保険の購入について同じ質問を自分自身に問いかけるでしょう。しかし、既に見てきたように、保険者が保険を購入する場合、これは「再保険」と呼ばれます。では、なぜ保険者は再保険を購入するのでしょうか？

paragraphs.text: Like insurance, reinsurance aims to reduce or eliminate the uncertainty of losses and provides peace of mind, and confidence to the direct insurer.
paragraphs.textJP: 保険と同様に、再保険は損失の不確実性を低減または排除し、元受保険者に安心と信頼を提供することを目的としています。

paragraphs.text: Reinsurance helps to stabilise the direct insurer’s losses by smoothing the fluctuations of the losses from year to year. The profitability of the direct insurer will then fluctuate less drastically, and this will help to stabilise the direct insurer’s financial performance.
paragraphs.textJP: 再保険は、年ごとの損失の変動を平準化することにより、元受保険者の損失を安定させるのに役立ちます。元受保険者の収益性の変動はより緩やかになり、元受保険者の財務業績の安定に寄与します。

paragraphs.text: Owing to its financial limitation, the direct insurer may not be able to accept risks that are beyond its underwriting capacity. By transferring part of the risks to the reinsurer, the direct insurer will be able to increase its capacity to accept more or larger risks for its own portfolio.
paragraphs.textJP: 財務的な制約のため、元受保険者は引受キャパシティを超えるリスクを引き受けることができない場合があります。リスクの一部を再保険者に移転することにより、元受保険者は自社のポートフォリオに対してより多くのまたはより大きなリスクを引き受けるキャパシティを増加させることができます。

paragraphs.text: In the event of a catastrophe, which can result in large volumes of claims and expensive individual claims, the financial resources of an insurer may be severely strained. By way of reinsurance, the effects of a catastrophe can be cushioned for the insurer.
paragraphs.textJP: 巨大災害が発生した場合、大量の保険金請求と高額な個別保険金請求が生じ、保険者の財務資源が深刻に圧迫される可能性があります。再保険により、巨大災害の影響を保険者にとって緩和することができます。

加えて、再保険者および／または再保険ブローカーは、出再保険会社に以下のようなアドバイザリーサービスも提供します。
▪ 再保険ニーズの定義と最も効果的な再保険プログラムの設計（出再保険者の資本ニーズとソルベンシー・マージンの計画改善のため）
▪ 出再保険者に対する技術研修、組織、会計、情報技術に関する幅広いサポートサービスの提供
▪ 複雑なリスクの分析、リスクモデリング、リスクプライシングなどの高度に専門化された分野における専門知識の提供
▪ 出再保険会社が一時的に資本不足であっても事業を構築できるよう支援すること（特にキャパシティを必要とする新しい保険商品の立ち上げのため）

paragraphs.text: Note: Reinsurance does not discharge the ceding insurance company from its liability to its policyholders.
paragraphs.textJP: 注：再保険は、出再保険会社の保険契約者に対する責任を免除するものではありません。

paragraphs.text: There are basically two methods of reinsurance, namely facultative and treaty.
paragraphs.textJP: 基本的に2つの再保険の方法があります。すなわち、任意再保険と特約再保険です。

paragraphs.text: Under this arrangement, the ceding insurance company reinsures or cedes each risk or policy on an individual basis. The ceding insurance company is at liberty to decide which risk that it wants to reinsure, how much it wants to be reinsured, and how much it will retain for itself. It also has the freedom to offer reinsurance to any reinsurer that it wishes. Similarly, the reinsurer is under no obligation to underwrite the risk being offered, i.e. it is at liberty to decline the risk, or write a share, if it sees fit.
paragraphs.textJP: この取り決めの下では、出再保険会社は各リスクまたは保険契約を個別に出再します。出再保険会社は、どのリスクを出再したいか、どの程度の出再を望むか、自社でどの程度保有するかを自由に決定できます。また、希望する任意の再保険者に再保険を提供する自由も有します。同様に、再保険者は提供されたリスクを引き受ける義務はなく、リスクを辞退するか、適当と判断すればシェアを引き受ける自由があります。

paragraphs.text: To place facultative reinsurance, the ceding insurance company must make available to the reinsurer all the relevant underwriting details. As such, this method of placement is generally time-consuming and costly to administer both for the ceding insurer and reinsurers. It is usually sought for risks that are considered more complex or hazardous and/or those that are beyond the insurer’s financial capacity.
paragraphs.textJP: 任意再保険を手配するためには、出再保険会社は関連する引受詳細のすべてを再保険者に提供しなければなりません。このため、この手配方法は一般に、出再保険者と再保険者の双方にとって時間とコストがかかります。通常、より複雑または危険なリスク、および／または保険者の財務的キャパシティを超えるリスクについて求められます。

paragraphs.text: Facultative reinsurance may be placed on a proportional basis (also called Proportional Facultative Reinsurance), or on a non-proportional basis (usually known as Facultative Excess of Loss) – see later sections of this chapter.
paragraphs.textJP: 任意再保険は、比例ベース（比例任意再保険とも呼ばれます）、または非比例ベース（通常、任意超過損害再保険として知られます）で手配されることがあります。本章の後のセクションを参照してください。

paragraphs.text: Let us consider an example. An energy company in Singapore, SG Energy Ltd, wishes to insure its assets. So, SG Energy Ltd approaches its insurance broker and asks the broker to arrange insurance. A local insurer, Insurer X underwrites the risk 100%. However, the risk is very large and so Insurer X arranged facultative reinsurance with 3 different reinsurers. In this example, Insurer X is the ceding insurer. The reinsurers agree to underwrite the following amounts of reinsurance: Reinsurer 1: 25% Reinsurer 2: 15% Reinsurer 3: 10%
paragraphs.textJP:
例を考えてみましょう。シンガポールのエネルギー会社SG Energy Ltdが資産を付保したいと考えています。SG Energy Ltdは保険ブローカーに依頼し、保険の手配を求めます。地場の保険者であるInsurer Xがリスクの100%を引き受けます。しかし、リスクは非常に大きいため、Insurer Xは3社の再保険者と任意再保険を手配しました。この例では、Insurer Xが出再保険者です。再保険者は以下の額の再保険を引き受けることに合意しました。
Reinsurer 1：25%
Reinsurer 2：15%
Reinsurer 3：10%

paragraphs.text: So, Insurer X will keep 50% of the risk and has arranged facultative reinsurance for 50% of the risk which can be seen in the following Diagram 8.3: Diagram 8.3: Facultative Reinsurance Process
paragraphs.textJP:
したがって、Insurer Xはリスクの50%を保有し、リスクの50%について任意再保険を手配したことになります。以下のDiagram 8.3に示されています。
Diagram 8.3：任意再保険プロセス

paragraphs.text: This is an agreement by which one or more reinsurers will automatically accept all reinsurance risks which fall within the pre-determined terms and limits. Most treaties are “blind”. This means that the reinsurers are bound to accept risks, without prior knowledge. The reinsurer cannot decline the risks that fall within the treaty, nor can the insurer select which risks that it can retain for its own account.
paragraphs.textJP: これは、1つ以上の再保険者が事前に決定された条件と限度額の範囲内にあるすべての再保険リスクを自動的に引き受ける合意です。ほとんどの特約は「ブラインド」です。これは、再保険者が事前の知識なしにリスクを引き受ける義務があることを意味します。再保険者は特約の範囲内にあるリスクを辞退することはできず、保険者も自社勘定で保有するリスクを選択することはできません。

paragraphs.text: This method of placement is administratively less cumbersome and less costly, as compared to the facultative method.
paragraphs.textJP: この手配方法は、任意再保険の方法と比較して、管理上の負担が少なくコストも低くなります。

paragraphs.text: Treaty reinsurance can be divided into two categories called “Proportional Reinsurance” and “Non-Proportional Reinsurance” which we shall now consider:
paragraphs.textJP: 特約再保険は、「比例再保険」と「非比例再保険」の2つのカテゴリーに分けられます。これから検討しましょう。

paragraphs.text: In proportional treaty reinsurance, the ceding insurer agrees to pass x% of all premium relating to a class of business to one or more reinsurers and the reinsurers agree to accept x% of the entire book of business.
paragraphs.textJP: 比例特約再保険では、出再保険者はある事業種類に関連するすべての保険料のx%を1つ以上の再保険者に移転することに合意し、再保険者は事業全体のx%を引き受けることに合意します。

paragraphs.text: Let us consider an example. Insurer X underwrites property, motor and liability insurance. Insurer X decides to enter into a proportional treaty reinsurance agreement with Reinsurer Y in relation to their motor account. Insurer X agrees to cede 30% of all premium to Reinsurer Y and Reinsurer Y agrees to accept 30% of all premium and pay 30% of all claims.
paragraphs.textJP: 例を考えてみましょう。Insurer Xは財物保険、自動車保険、賠償責任保険を引き受けています。Insurer Xは自動車保険勘定について、Reinsurer Yと比例特約再保険契約を締結することを決定しました。Insurer Xはすべての保険料の30%をReinsurer Yに出再することに合意し、Reinsurer Yはすべての保険料の30%を引き受け、すべての保険金請求の30%を支払うことに合意しました。

paragraphs.text: There are different types of Proportional Treaty Reinsurance, the most common of which we have described in our example above: this type of Proportional Treaty Reinsurance is called “Quota Share” treaty reinsurance. Diagram 8.4: Proportional Reinsurance: Quota Share Process
paragraphs.textJP:
比例特約再保険にはさまざまな種類がありますが、上の例で説明した最も一般的なものは「クォータシェア」特約再保険と呼ばれます。
Diagram 8.4：比例再保険：クォータシェアプロセス

paragraphs.text: Before we consider Non-Proportional Reinsurance let us remind ourselves about an important concept of insurance. It is common within insurance for policyholders to carry an excess. If you own a car and drive on the roads, then you must purchase motor insurance by law. Your motor insurance policy may carry an excess of, say S$500 and this means that if you have a claim then you must pay the first S$500 of each claim. Insurers impose an excess to encourage good risk management; in this example the policyholder has a vested interest in driving carefully and avoiding accidents as he will have to pay the first S$500 himself if he causes an accident.
paragraphs.textJP: 非比例再保険について検討する前に、保険の重要な概念について思い出しましょう。保険では、保険契約者がエクセスを負担することが一般的です。自動車を所有し公道を走行する場合、法律により自動車保険を購入しなければなりません。自動車保険にはS$500のエクセスが付されていることがあり、これは保険金請求がある場合に各請求の最初のS$500を自己負担しなければならないことを意味します。保険者は良好なリスク管理を奨励するためにエクセスを課しています。

paragraphs.text: Similarly, Insurer X may wish to protect itself against large claims and it might want to purchase reinsurance to help with this. This type of reinsurance is available, but reinsurers will impose an excess to encourage good risk selection on the part of the insurer. Insurance companies purchasing Non-Proportional Reinsurance will need to pay the first S$x themselves. Some larger insurers may pay the first S$50 Million themselves before purchasing Non-Proportional Reinsurance (i.e. their excess is S$50 Million). Smaller insurers may have an excess of S$500,000.
paragraphs.textJP: 同様に、Insurer Xは大規模な保険金請求から身を守りたいと考え、そのための再保険を購入したいかもしれません。このような再保険は利用可能ですが、再保険者は保険者側の良好なリスク選択を奨励するためにエクセスを課します。非比例再保険を購入する保険会社は、最初のS$xを自己負担しなければなりません。大規模な保険者は非比例再保険を購入する前にS$5,000万を自己負担する場合があり（エクセスがS$5,000万）、小規模な保険者のエクセスはS$500,000の場合があります。

paragraphs.text: Let us imagine that Insurer X wants to protect itself against all claims in excess of S$1 Million. Insurer X is likely to purchase a type of Non-Proportional reinsurance called “Excess of Loss” meaning that the reinsurer(s) pay all amounts above the excess. So, if there is a claim for S$900,000 then insurer X must pay S$900,000. However, if there is a claim in excess of S1 Million then Insurer X pays S$1 Million and the reinsurer(s) pay all amounts above S$1 Million. If there was a valid claim of S$3 Million, then Insurer X would initially pay S$3 Million to the policyholder but could recover S$2 Million from the reinsurer(s).
paragraphs.textJP: Insurer XがS$100万を超えるすべての保険金請求から身を守りたいと想像してみましょう。Insurer Xは「超過損害（Excess of Loss）」と呼ばれる非比例再保険を購入する可能性が高いです。これは、再保険者がエクセスを超えるすべての金額を支払うことを意味します。S$90万の保険金請求がある場合、Insurer XはS$90万を支払わなければなりません。しかし、S$100万を超える保険金請求がある場合、Insurer XはS$100万を支払い、再保険者がS$100万を超えるすべての金額を支払います。S$300万の有効な保険金請求があった場合、Insurer Xは当初保険契約者にS$300万を支払いますが、再保険者からS$200万を回収することができます。

paragraphs.text: There are different types of Non-Proportional Reinsurance, the most common of which we have described in our example: “Excess of Loss” reinsurance.
paragraphs.textJP: 非比例再保険にはさまざまな種類がありますが、上の例で説明した最も一般的なものは「超過損害（Excess of Loss）」再保険です。

paragraphs.text: Let us look at an example where the use of a diagram will help to illustrate the concept. In the example below, Insurer X has selected an excess of S$1 Million in relation to its motor book. Insurer X has received 5 claims which it has paid the amounts stated below: Claim 1: S$400,000 Claim 2: S$1,400,000 Claim 3: S$2,000,000 Claim 4: S$2,500,000 Claim 5: S$3,000,000
paragraphs.textJP:
図表を用いて概念を説明する例を見てみましょう。以下の例では、Insurer Xは自動車保険勘定についてS$100万のエクセスを選択しました。Insurer Xは以下の金額を支払った5件の保険金請求を受けました。
Claim 1：S$400,000
Claim 2：S$1,400,000
Claim 3：S$2,000,000
Claim 4：S$2,500,000
Claim 5：S$3,000,000

subsections.label: B3
subsections.title: Non-Proportional Reinsurance: Excess Of Loss
subsections.titleJP: 非比例再保険：超過損害（Excess of Loss）

paragraphs.text: Diagram 8.5: Excess Of Loss
paragraphs.textJP: Diagram 8.5：超過損害（Excess of Loss）

paragraphs.text: In these examples, Insurer X is obliged to pay the claims in full, but can they recover anything from their Non-Proportional Excess of Loss reinsurer(s)? The answer is shown in the following table and illustrated by the diagram above. Claim 1 S$400,000 Claim falls within the excess so Insurer X cannot recover anything. Claim 2 S$1,400,000 Claim exceeds the excess of S$1,000,000 by S$400,000 so Insurer X can recover S$400,000. Claim 3 S$2,000,000 Claim exceeds the excess of S$1,000,000 by S$1,000,000 so Insurer X can recover S$1,000,000. Claim 4 S$2,500,000 Claim exceeds the excess of S$1,000,000 by S$1,500,000 so Insurer X can recover S$1,500,000. Claim 5 S$3,000,000 Claim exceeds the excess of S$1m by S$2,000,000 so Insurer X can recover S$2,000,000
paragraphs.textJP:
これらの例では、Insurer Xは保険金請求を全額支払う義務がありますが、非比例超過損害再保険者から何かを回収できるでしょうか？ 答えは以下の表に示され、上の図で説明されています。
Claim 1 S$400,000 — 保険金請求はエクセスの範囲内のため、Insurer Xは回収できません。
Claim 2 S$1,400,000 — 保険金請求がエクセスS$1,000,000をS$400,000超過しているため、Insurer XはS$400,000を回収できます。
Claim 3 S$2,000,000 — エクセスをS$1,000,000超過しているため、Insurer XはS$1,000,000を回収できます。
Claim 4 S$2,500,000 — エクセスをS$1,500,000超過しているため、Insurer XはS$1,500,000を回収できます。
Claim 5 S$3,000,000 — エクセスをS$2,000,000超過しているため、Insurer XはS$2,000,000を回収できます。

paragraphs.text: Co-insurance usually occurs when the insurer finds that the sum insured is above its normal acceptance limit or that the broker decides it wants to place the risk with several insurers. Sometimes, the insurance market uses the term “collective policy” to describe a co-insurance policy.
paragraphs.textJP: 共同保険は通常、保険者が保険金額が通常の引受限度を超えていると判断した場合、またはブローカーが複数の保険者にリスクを分散して付託することを決定した場合に行われます。保険市場では共同保険証券を「共同保険証券（collective policy）」と呼ぶこともあります。

paragraphs.text: Under a co-insurance agreement, rather than each insurer issuing its own policy, one insurance policy will be issued to the policyholder. One insurer is nominated as the “leading office” or “leading insurer” of the panel of insurers covering the risk. Usually, the insurer with the largest percentage of the risk is nominated to be the leading office.
paragraphs.textJP: 共同保険の取り決めの下では、各保険者が独自の保険証券を発行するのではなく、1通の保険証券が保険契約者に発行されます。リスクをカバーする保険者パネルの「幹事保険者」として1社の保険者が指名されます。通常、リスクの最大割合を持つ保険者が幹事保険者に指名されます。

paragraphs.text: The leading insurer will assume responsibility for all negotiations regarding the insurance cover. This includes the arrangement of any required survey, the calculation of the premium, and the issue of the production of the policy and any subsequent endorsements.
paragraphs.textJP: 幹事保険者は、保険カバーに関するすべての交渉について責任を負います。これには、必要な調査の手配、保険料の計算、保険証券の作成・発行、およびその後の裏書が含まれます。

paragraphs.text: The panel of insurers will verify that the premium charged and policy terms match those that they would have offered should they have been the leading office.
paragraphs.textJP: 保険者パネルは、課される保険料と保険証券の条件が、自社が幹事保険者であった場合に提供したであろうものと一致することを確認します。

paragraphs.text: The leading office and panel of insurers will share the premium and any subsequent claims in proportion to their percentage share of the risk.
paragraphs.textJP: 幹事保険者と保険者パネルは、リスクの割合に応じて保険料とその後の保険金請求を分担します。

subsections.label: A
subsections.title: Relationships In Shared Insurances
subsections.titleJP: 共有保険における関係

paragraphs.text: Insurance, co-insurance, and reinsurance create different relationships between the parties involved in them.
paragraphs.textJP: 保険、共同保険、再保険は、関係する当事者間に異なる関係を作り出します。

paragraphs.text: Where there is only the involvement of the insured and the insurer there is a direct relationship. Within this relationship the insured must reveal to the insurer all material facts relevant to the risk to allow the insurer to correctly assess the risk and decide whether it is to be accepted, develop the terms and conditions for the risk, and calculate the premium. The insurer also has a duty of disclosure and should advise the proposer if they are unable to underwrite the risk or that there is any other difficulties in providing insurance cover, for example, if the insurer is not authorised to write that class of business.
paragraphs.textJP: 被保険者と保険者のみが関与する場合、直接的な関係があります。この関係の中で、被保険者はリスクに関連するすべての重要事実を保険者に告知し、保険者がリスクを正しく評価し、引き受けるかどうかを決定し、リスクの条件を策定し、保険料を計算できるようにしなければなりません。保険者にも告知義務があり、リスクを引き受けることができないか、保険カバーの提供に何らかの困難がある場合（例えば、保険者がその事業種類を引き受ける権限がない場合）は申込者に助言すべきです。

paragraphs.text: Under a direct insurer and insured relationship, the insurer is entirely responsible to pay any claim under the insurance arranged.
paragraphs.textJP: 元受保険者と被保険者の直接的な関係の下では、保険者は手配された保険に基づく保険金請求を全額支払う責任を負います。

paragraphs.text: Under a reinsurance arrangement, the insurer and its reinsurer have a direct relationship. Therefore, under a reinsurance arrangement the insurer must advise the reinsurer of any facts that are relevant to the risk to be reinsured.
paragraphs.textJP: 再保険の取り決めの下では、保険者とその再保険者は直接的な関係を有します。したがって、再保険の取り決めの下では、保険者は出再されるリスクに関連するあらゆる事実を再保険者に通知しなければなりません。

paragraphs.text:
This is to allow the reinsurer to evaluate whether to accept all or part of the risk offered. The reinsurer will make similar decisions on the acceptance of a risk that an insurer would make, including:
▪ Whether the rates and terms are adequate?
▪ What acceptance class applies to this risk?
▪ What limits can be written?
paragraphs.textJP:
これは、再保険者が提供されたリスクの全部または一部を引き受けるかどうかを評価するためです。再保険者は、保険者が行うのと同様のリスクの引受判断を行います。
▪ 料率と条件は十分か
▪ このリスクに適用される引受区分は何か
▪ どのような限度額を設定できるか

paragraphs.text: The question of the reinsurer’s solvency is key to the insurer as they rely on the solvency of the reinsurer to allow the payment of any future claims. The importance of this issue is that if the reinsurer goes out of business, the reinsurer will be unable to pay their proportion of the claim to the insurer. This would mean that the insurer could have to pay an amount which they had anticipated would be reinsured. As shown in the diagram above, Insurer X expects to collect S$400,000 in respect of claim 2, plus S$1 Million in respect of claim 3, S$1.5 Million in respect of claim 4 and S$2 Million in respect of claim 5. In total, Insurer X expects to recover S$4.9 Million in respect of these claims. However, if the reinsurer goes out of business then Insurer X will be unable to recover this amount of S$4.9 Million.
paragraphs.textJP: 再保険者の支払能力の問題は、保険者にとって重要です。保険者は将来の保険金請求の支払いを可能にするために再保険者の支払能力に依存しているからです。この問題の重要性は、再保険者が廃業した場合、再保険者は保険者に対するその持分の保険金を支払うことができなくなることです。保険者は再保険されることを見込んでいた金額を支払わなければならなくなる可能性があります。

paragraphs.text: There is no direct relationship between an insured and a reinsurer. The insured has no legal entitlement on any outstanding loss payment due from the reinsurer to the insurer if the insurer goes out of business before a claim is paid. In such a situation, the insured would need to seek recovery of its claim payment as a creditor during the process of winding up the insurer.
paragraphs.textJP: 被保険者と再保険者の間には直接的な関係はありません。保険者が保険金請求の支払い前に廃業した場合、被保険者は再保険者から保険者への未払い損失金について法的権利を有しません。このような状況では、被保険者は保険者の清算手続き中に債権者として保険金の回収を求めなければなりません。

paragraphs.text: There is a direct relationship between the insured and the co-insurer. In fact, the relationship is no different to that of an insured and insurer. However, the limit of the co-insurers liability to the insured is its percentage of the co-insurance written.
paragraphs.textJP: 被保険者と共同保険者の間には直接的な関係があります。実際、この関係は被保険者と保険者の関係と何ら異なるものではありません。ただし、被保険者に対する共同保険者の責任の限度は、引き受けた共同保険の割合です。

paragraphs.text: Therefore, if a co-insurer goes out of business and cannot fulfil its claims obligations, then their portion of that policy effectively “dies”, and the remaining co-insurers have no responsibility for the share of that co-insurer. An example will better illustrate this concept.
paragraphs.textJP: したがって、共同保険者が廃業し保険金請求の義務を果たせない場合、その保険証券のその持分は実質的に「消滅」し、残りの共同保険者はその共同保険者の持分について責任を負いません。例を挙げて説明します。

paragraphs.text: Example: An insurance broker arranges a policy on behalf of its policyholder, which is co-insured by five insurers. The insurers co-insure in these proportions: Insurer Co-insurance Share Share of Claim of S$1,000,000 A 50% S$500,000 B 30% S$300,000 C 10% S$100,000 D 7% S$ 70,000 E 3% S$ 30,000 Total 100% S$1,000,000
paragraphs.textJP:
例：保険ブローカーが保険契約者に代わって保険証券を手配し、5社の保険者が共同保険しています。保険者は以下の割合で共同保険します。
保険者 | 共同保険の割合 | S$1,000,000の保険金請求の持分
A | 50% | S$500,000
B | 30% | S$300,000
C | 10% | S$100,000
D | 7% | S$70,000
E | 3% | S$30,000
合計 | 100% | S$1,000,000

paragraphs.text: This can also be illustrated diagrammatically as follows: Diagram 8.6: Example Of Co-Insurer Proportions
paragraphs.textJP:
これは以下のように図式的にも示すことができます。
Diagram 8.6：共同保険者の割合の例

paragraphs.text: If the policyholder has a valid claim in the sum of S$1,000,000, then the amounts recoverable from each insurer are shown in the table above.
paragraphs.textJP: 保険契約者がS$1,000,000の有効な保険金請求を有する場合、各保険者から回収可能な金額は上の表に示されています。

paragraphs.text: If Insurer C goes out of business before the claim is paid, then the policyholder will be unable to recover that proportion of the claim amounting to S$100,000. In such circumstances the policyholder will only recover S$900,000 rather than the agreed settlement amount of S$1,000,000.
paragraphs.textJP: 保険金請求が支払われる前にInsurer Cが廃業した場合、保険契約者はS$100,000に相当するその持分の保険金を回収することができなくなります。このような場合、保険契約者は合意された決済額S$1,000,000ではなくS$900,000のみを回収することになります。

sections.title: Settlement Of Claims Under Co-insurance Policies
sections.titleJP: 共同保険における保険金請求の決済

paragraphs.text: At times, owing to the size of the sum insured or complexity of the risk involved, it may be necessary to place insurance with two or more insurers. As this chapter discussed earlier, one method of achieving this is via co-insurance, or issuing a collective policy.
paragraphs.textJP: 保険金額の大きさやリスクの複雑さのため、2社以上の保険者に保険を付託する必要がある場合があります。本章で前述したように、これを実現する方法の一つは共同保険、すなわち共同保険証券の発行です。

paragraphs.text: Co-insurance is where a substantial insurance is shared among several different insurance companies. A single policy is prepared which shows the different companies, described in the document as co-insurers, and the percentages for each co-insurer’s share of the risk. Each insurer is responsible only for its agreed percentage of any loss and liability is several but not joint. This is in contrast to reinsurance where the insurer is still liable to the insured even if the reinsurer should go into liquidation.
paragraphs.textJP: 共同保険とは、大規模な保険がいくつかの異なる保険会社の間で分担されることです。各社の割合を示す単一の保険証券が作成されます。各保険者はその合意された割合の損失についてのみ責任を負い、責任は個別責任であって連帯責任ではありません。これは、再保険者が清算に入ったとしても保険者が被保険者に対して引き続き責任を負う再保険とは対照的です。

paragraphs.text: The “leader”, usually the insurer with the largest percentage, assumes responsibility for correspondence, surveys, rating and the preparation of the closing instructions and specification which are despatched to co-insurers. So, in our example above, the leading insurer would be Insurer A.
paragraphs.textJP: 「幹事」は通常最大の割合を持つ保険者であり、通信、調査、料率設定、クロージング指示書および仕様書の作成の責任を負い、これらを共同保険者に送付します。上の例では、幹事保険者はInsurer Aとなります。

paragraphs.text: The closing instructions carry details of the sum insured and premium to enable the co-insurers to place the insurance on their books.
paragraphs.textJP: クロージング指示書には、共同保険者が自社の帳簿に保険を計上するための保険金額と保険料の詳細が記載されます。

paragraphs.text: The co-insurers signify their agreement to the terms of the policy by returning a “signing slip” authorising the leader to sign the policy on their behalf.
paragraphs.textJP: 共同保険者は、幹事保険者がその代理として保険証券に署名することを認める「サイニングスリップ」を返送することにより、保険証券の条件への同意を表明します。

paragraphs.text: Following a loss, the leader usually appoints a loss adjuster who issue either a preliminary advice or a brief preliminary report to all co-insurers. The loss adjuster will send the final report to the leader for approval when the loss adjustment has been completed. If everything is in order, the leader authorises the loss adjuster to issue copies of the report and apportionment to the coinsurers, and to tell them the date on which they should all simultaneously pay the insured or the intermediary, if one is involved.
paragraphs.textJP: 損失の発生後、幹事保険者は通常損害査定人を任命し、損害査定人はすべての共同保険者に予備的通知または簡潔な予備報告書を発行します。損害査定が完了すると、損害査定人は承認のために幹事保険者に最終報告書を送付します。すべてが問題なければ、幹事保険者は損害査定人に報告書と按分表のコピーを共同保険者に発行し、すべての保険者が同時に被保険者または仲介者（関与する場合）に支払うべき日付を通知することを認可します。

paragraphs.text: Each co-insurer has a separate contract with the insured. Should a co-insurer disagree with the leader’s recommendations, it may voice its concerns and raise points which it considers relevant.
paragraphs.textJP: 各共同保険者は被保険者と個別の契約を有しています。共同保険者が幹事保険者の勧告に同意しない場合、懸念を表明し、関連があると考える点を提起することができます。

paragraphs.text: When a claim is agreed, co-insurers usually make their own individual payments at a date specified for payment detailed in the loss adjuster’s report.
paragraphs.textJP: 保険金請求が合意されると、共同保険者は通常、損害査定人の報告書に記載された支払日に個別に支払いを行います。

paragraphs.text: It is possible that a co-insurer declines to follow the leader’s recommendations for payment of a loss. In this situation, the insured can proceed against the relevant co-insurer to obtain payment. However, where the loss adjusters feel that the loss is not payable or there is some issue with the loss in question, the leader will often seek the views of the co-insurers.
paragraphs.textJP: 共同保険者が幹事保険者の損失支払いの勧告に従うことを拒否する可能性があります。このような場合、被保険者は支払いを得るために当該共同保険者に対して訴訟を提起することができます。ただし、損害査定人が損失が支払い不能であるか、問題のある損失であると判断した場合、幹事保険者はしばしば共同保険者の意見を求めます。

sections.title: Comparison Between Reinsurance & Co-insurance
sections.titleJP: 再保険と共同保険の比較

paragraphs.text: Co-insurance is a method, whereby one insurer (known as the leading office or primary insurer) shares direct responsibility for a risk with one or more insurance companies (known as co-insurers) on a proportional basis. As such, several insurers are usually involved, each taking a stated proportion of the risk, each receiving that proportion of the premiums, and each being responsible for that proportion of any claim made under the policy. The lead insurer administers the co-insurance arrangement.
paragraphs.textJP: 共同保険は、1つの保険者（幹事保険者または主保険者と呼ばれます）が1つ以上の保険会社（共同保険者と呼ばれます）と比例ベースでリスクに対する直接的な責任を分担する方法です。したがって、通常、複数の保険者が関与し、各保険者がリスクの所定の割合を引き受け、保険料のその割合を受け取り、保険証券に基づいてなされた保険金請求のその割合について責任を負います。幹事保険者が共同保険の取り決めを管理します。

paragraphs.text: Co-insurance is fairly common for large risks. The method of operating coinsurance procedures is straightforward and suitable for many risks. However, there are some drawbacks. The first concern is that, in the event of a large loss, all the co-insurers make their individual payments at different times. This can be cumbersome if many insurance companies are involved.
paragraphs.textJP: 共同保険は大規模なリスクについてかなり一般的です。共同保険の手続きの運営方法は簡単明瞭であり、多くのリスクに適しています。しかし、いくつかの欠点があります。第一の懸念は、大規模な損失の場合、すべての共同保険者が異なる時期に個別に支払いを行うことです。多くの保険会社が関与する場合、これは煩雑になりえます。

paragraphs.text:
The second disadvantage is that a broker, in placing a very large or complicated risk, will need to approach many different insurers to complete the placement. As discussed earlier, the concept of reinsurance is different from the concept of co-insurance. Table 8.1 below summarises the main differences. Table 8.1: Difference Between Reinsurance & Co-insurance Reinsurance Co-insurance
▪ Reinsurance involves one insurer covering the risk and insuring again (“re-insuring”) part or parts of it with reinsurer(s). As mentioned before, the insurance contract is between the insurer and the insured with the reinsurer(s) not being a party to this insurance contract.
▪ Co-insurance implies that two or more insurers directly insure the risk, so that the insurers share the risk.
▪ The reinsurance contract is between the insurer and the reinsurer with the insured not being a party to it. This means that if a reinsurer fails, the insurer is still liable to the insured under the terms of the insurance contract, notwithstanding that the insurer is unable to recover the share of the loss from the reinsurer.
▪ The insured will have a separate insurance contract with each coinsurer, albeit there is only one insurance policy issued by the leading office, with the identities and respective percentage shares of each co-insurer indicated. Therefore, the insured has a direct relationship with each insurer for its share of the risk. Should any of the coinsurers fail, the other coinsurers are liable to the insured only for their respective proportionate shares of the loss. They are not liable for the failed co-insurer’s share.
paragraphs.textJP:
第二の欠点は、ブローカーが非常に大きなまたは複雑なリスクを付託する際に、配分を完了するために多くの異なる保険者に接触する必要があることです。前述のとおり、再保険の概念は共同保険の概念とは異なります。以下のTable 8.1は主な違いを要約しています。
Table 8.1：再保険と共同保険の違い
再保険 | 共同保険
▪ 再保険は、1つの保険者がリスクをカバーし、その一部を再保険者に再保険（「re-insure」）することを含みます。保険契約は保険者と被保険者の間のものであり、再保険者はこの保険契約の当事者ではありません。
▪ 共同保険は、2つ以上の保険者がリスクを直接付保し、保険者がリスクを分担することを意味します。
▪ 再保険契約は保険者と再保険者の間のものであり、被保険者はそれの当事者ではありません。これは、再保険者が破綻した場合、保険者は保険契約の条件に基づき被保険者に対して依然として責任を負うことを意味します。再保険者から損失の持分を回収できないにもかかわらずです。
▪ 被保険者は各共同保険者と個別の保険契約を有します（ただし、幹事保険者が発行する保険証券は1通のみであり、各共同保険者の名称とそれぞれの割合が記載されます）。したがって、被保険者はリスクの持分について各保険者と直接的な関係を有します。共同保険者のいずれかが破綻した場合、他の共同保険者はそれぞれの比例的持分についてのみ被保険者に対して責任を負います。破綻した共同保険者の持分について責任を負うことはありません。

paragraphs.text: The following Diagram 8.7 illustrates the difference between co-insurance and reinsurance: Diagram 8.7: Difference Between Co-Insurance And Reinsurance
paragraphs.textJP:
以下のDiagram 8.7は、共同保険と再保険の違いを示しています。
Diagram 8.7：共同保険と再保険の違い

paragraphs.text: The diagram above illustrates the difference between co-insurance and reinsurance. You will see that, in each case, the leading office or primary insurer holds 40% of the risks – the difference arises in the relationship among the parties.
paragraphs.textJP: 上の図は共同保険と再保険の違いを示しています。いずれの場合も、幹事保険者または主保険者がリスクの40%を保有していることがわかります。違いは当事者間の関係において生じます。

chapters.title: ETHICS, PROFESSIONALISM, DATA PROTECTION AND CYBER HYGIENE
outline: Why Ethics Is Important To The Insurance Industry
The Singapore General Insurance Code Of Practice
Singapore Insurance Brokers’ Association Code Of Conduct 2023
Personal Data Protection And Cyber Hygiene
learningOutcomes: understand what ethics is and why ethics is not compliance
understand why ethics is important to the insurance industry
understand the general ethical principles that agents and brokers should adhere to
recognise the importance of courage in adhering to the ethical principles
know the benefits of ethical behaviour and be mindful of some unethical acts
identify the characteristics of professionals and the requirements for a profession
know the responsibilities of professionals
outline the Singapore General Insurance Code of Practice, the Code of Practice For Agents and Singapore Insurance Brokers’ Association Code of Conduct
explain the importance of good conduct in relation to dealing with insurers and clients
know the Personal Data Protection Act 2012, its provisions and enforcement
understand the requirements of Notice No: MAS 132 on Cyber Hygiene
know the Data Loss Protection Guidelines (DPL) For Life & General Insurance Agents

paragraphs.text:
Ethics is both a field of study and a skill. As a field of study, it is a branch of philosophy that investigates how we should behave. Specifically, people who study ethics focus on three questions:
▪ What does it mean to characterise an action as “good” or a person as “good”?
▪ What do we owe to other people?
▪ How should we respond when the obligations which we owe to others (and ourselves) come into conflict? In other words, which obligation takes priority?

Applied ethics, or ethics as a skill, is the ability to apply our moral beliefs of what is “good” and “bad” to situations that we face each day. Ethics, in the business arena, is about how people conduct business. Ethics is the strong base that holds the entire economic and free enterprise system together. Without ethical behaviour, business deals will collapse, the working environment will be intolerable, and trust will be non-existent. In business activities, we act based on trust that our business associates will act ethically. To be an ethical person, one must not only think ethically but must also act in this manner. Ethical behaviour implies doing what is right, and that is sufficient justification.

Law and ethics are both standards of conduct that govern a country, the morality of an organisation and the moral actions of individuals. However, in the next section, we outline why ethics is not compliance.

Ethics, however, is not compliance. Fulfilling legal obligations does not exhaust moral obligations. Ethics requires more than compliance with a set of rules and regulations. An ethical person is one who applies his moral beliefs, in the form of principles and values, to his business practice, because he believes that it is good to do so; and the right thing to do.

sections.title: Why Ethics Is Important To The Insurance Industry

subsections.label: A
subsections.title: Insurance Business Is Based On Trust

paragraphs.text: The insurance industry is one that is based on trust. Insurance products, unlike consumer products, are intangible and carry benefits that may not be apparent until a covered risk occurs. One cannot enjoy or display “financial security” or “risk protection” in the way that one can with a new house or a new car.

The client cannot always measure the value and suitability of the insurance products and advice that they purchase. They are not sure whether the advice that they are paying for is necessarily all that good, or that the products that they purchase are the best fit. This is because insurance products are complex, and policy wordings are hard to understand. As a result, policy benefits may be misrepresented, the products sold may not meet the needs of the clients, and the clients may not fully understand what they are buying. The clients may suffer financial distress if they do not receive proper advice. Increasingly more and more insurers are using simple layman’s English in their policy wordings. This is an important part of ethical sales behaviour, which is extremely crucial for the insurance sector.

subsections.label: B
subsections.title: Ethics Affects The Industry’s Public Image

paragraphs.text: Insurance is a public-interest industry, meaning that the insurance industry is not just in business for itself – the public obtains substantial benefit from the availability of insurance. The unethical behaviour of a few can tarnish the image of the industry and cause a loss of public confidence. The reputation of an entire industry is at stake if we tolerate unethical sales behaviour or unethical business practices.

Obviously, the specific applications of ethics will be varied, but there are certain general ethical principles that can be borne in mind, whether one’s code of ethics is internally generated or externally prescribed by a recognised authority.

It may be helpful to consider these general principles by looking at each of the letters in the word “ETHICS” and making an appropriate application (of course, not necessarily in order of importance).
Excellence is the quality of being outstanding. To excel, to be the best that one can be, will certainly require ethical behaviour.

Gains obtained through unethical actions are lost eventually, and along with them reputation, self-esteem and trust. Perhaps the means of making one’s livelihood will also be lost. On the other hand, ethical actions generate respect and confidence from clients and colleagues. In the general insurance business, ethical behaviour and excellence certainly go hand in hand. E-xcellence T-rustworthiness H-onesty I-ntegrity C-aring S-elflessness

To be a responsible agent/broker, one must be reliable and trustworthy. This is an essential element in the ethics of the professional general insurance agent/broker because, as we have said before, the general insurance business is built on trust.

The general insurance agent/broker possesses specialised technical knowledge of insurance products that an average insurance buyer does not usually possess. As a result, insurance buyers must rely on agents/brokers for their professional expertise. General insurance agents/brokers have ethical obligations to accept and fulfil their responsibilities to the best of their abilities.
Honesty is a cornerstone of ethical behaviour and means “telling the truth.” Someone who is honest takes care not to deceive others, either by what they say, or what they fail to say.

For example, suppose an agent has told a client that the policy covers accidental death on a 24-hour worldwide basis, we will not consider that agent honest, if the policy excludes accidents occurring in the United States of America and Canada, since the agent has not clearly made known the exclusions to the client. The statement may have been accurate as far as it goes, but the agent has withheld a material fact which will likely result in a misunderstanding on the part of the client. Honesty requires telling the whole truth.

Besides being truthful, honesty also means being fair. Honesty means making sure that others receive what they are entitled to, and not accepting things to which one is not entitled. Clients pay for an objective evaluation of their general insurance needs, for an objective recommendation about what will best meet their needs, and for ongoing service to ensure that their needs are continually met, and they should get nothing less.

Being honest is essential to creating the kind of trust in the agent-client relationship that allows the client to make an affirmative and informed buying decision. Clients are not going to buy general insurance from an agent whom they think has been dishonest with them, nor will they refer that agent to other people whom they know. At the same time, clients are eager to work with agents whom they know have made a competent evaluation of their general insurance needs, as well as an objective recommendation on how they should meet those needs.
In addition, agents/brokers who handle themselves in an honest and professional manner have no trouble in obtaining referrals to other high-quality prospects.

Integrity is similar to honesty, but integrity carries with it the connotation of being incorruptible, no matter how great the temptation is to be dishonest. A person who has integrity does the right thing, regardless of the consequences. Some people only want to be honest, as long as it does not cost them too much.

The price which these people are willing to pay varies. For example, for the sake of being honest, some may be willing to risk losing a small sale, but not a large one. Some may be willing to risk losing sales of any size, but not their job.

The higher degrees of honesty may be more commendable, but the highest degree of honesty, and the most commendable, is being willing to risk anything and everything for the sake of being honest. That is integrity.

In the short term, fraud, deception or theft may lead to greater profits than honesty and truthfulness. However, agents/brokers who are involved in illegal schemes, because they opt for short-term profits at the expense of the long successfully insurance careers and can tarnish the image of the insurance community. Those who lack personal integrity will not last very long in the business.

Having a caring attitude is the motivation behind the work of the professional general insurance agent/broker. No amount of money or recognition is rewarding enough for the challenges that general insurance agents/brokers must face day after day and year after year in their careers.

The real payoff is knowing that they have helped people with their business, managed risks and kept their financial houses in order: that individuals will have resources upon which they can count on when they are hospitalised or disabled; that there will be monetary compensation to help keep the businesses operating during disasters and catastrophes; that the community can continue to provide jobs and services to the society and benefit the economy as a whole, because the risks are contained through adequate and appropriate insurance.

Being caring also enables professional general insurance agents/brokers to act in their clients’ best interests. For, if agents/brokers care about their clients, they would do for their clients what they would do for themselves, as if they were in the clients’ situations.

Selflessness is the opposite of selfishness. Selfishness is concerned only with oneself and a disregard for others. One should readily see that the general insurance business is no place for the selfish person. General insurance agents/brokers have to put others first, because the purpose of the general insurance business is to be of service to others, to identify the risks, and to obtain and provide adequate cover for the risks which the client is facing. General insurance agents/brokers succeed the most by putting others first. By directly serving others’ best interests, the agents/brokers indirectly serve their own interests.

Agents who feel a sense of service towards their customers and principal companies, put someone else’s interest before their own. They set aside their own interests and concentrate on doing what has to be done in the best interest of their customers or prospective customers. In the long term, this will pay off for the agent in both material and non-material outcomes or results.
Besides the above six ethical principles, it also takes courage to be ethical. The right thing may always be the best thing in the long term, but in the short term, there may be a price to pay.

To be ethical, individuals may find that they have to stand up to a client, or to a colleague, or to a superior, or even to their family members, who do not want to risk the material loss upon coming to toe the ethical line. It takes courage to stand up to those persons whose expectations that we are ordinarily eager to meet.
All the good intentions in the world will not amount to anything, unless one acts on one’s principles. Courage is the quality that converts ethical intention into ethical action.

On the positive side, courage is a universally admired trait. When individuals demonstrate that they have the courage to stand up for their principles, they win the respect of their peers, their superiors, their clients, and their family members. Individuals who at first feel alone, when faced with an ethical situation requiring courage, often end up finding a great deal of support for having done the right thing.

One situation which takes courage for an insurance agent/broker is declining to work with a party with whom the insurance agent/broker feels that he cannot establish a mutually beneficial professional relationship. For example, an insurance agent/broker may be introduced to a client who does not value ethical behaviour. It is hard to turn down the possibility of making a sale. However, insurance agents/brokers must keep in mind that clients are likely to provide referrals to other people like themselves. It is better to give up one sale than to try to build a career out of ethically compromised actions. It is easier and more profitable for insurance agents/brokers to work with ethical people who will appreciate the value of their services, as well as their ethical orientation, and who will refer them to more people whose values that they share.

A truly professional insurance agent/broker will want to exhibit ethical behaviour, because he is convinced that the benefits of demonstrating ethical business behaviour far outweigh the costs of not doing so. What then are the benefits of ethical behaviour?

To people with high ethical standards, self-respect is more important than any reward that someone else can offer. The fact does not change even when unethical people seem to benefit from their behaviour, or when ethical behaviour goes unrecognised. Ethical people do the right thing, because to them, it is the right thing to do.
The honest man never needs to fear an audit. The dishonourable one lives in a state of constant worry that he will be found out and exposed.

Knowing that people trust you and rely on you for accurate advice, and act according to your advice, pertaining to their financial needs, has an enormous psychological influence, not to mention boosting one’s self-esteem.

The true ethical professional will obtain recognition from his profession. This may manifest itself in the insurance agent/broker’s increased profile in professional gatherings, or in being asked to assume office in professional associations, societies or clubs. All these will contribute to an increased awareness of your standing, which can only be to your professional advantage.

It is a fact that people like to do and continue to do business with someone whom they trust. A long-standing client is an automatic advertisement for the insurance agent/broker. If the client is satisfied with your service for the particular policy, you will well be the first in his thought, if he has other insurance needs that come to his mind.

Additionally, the client will not be slow in recommending family members, business associates, colleagues and friends to place their required insurance covers through you. The importance of repeat and growth business stemming from a satisfied client cannot be over-estimated. An insurance agent/broker rarely builds a successful career out of making one-time sales to strangers. Insurance agents/brokers need referrals, and repeat sales come only when a relationship of trust has been established between insurance agents/brokers and clients. Trust and reputation are built over time.

subsections.label: F
subsections.title: Boosting The Image Of The Insurance Community

paragraphs.text: By demonstrating ethical behaviour and building up a good reputation for himself, the insurance agent/broker also helps to develop the public’s sense of respect and trust for the insurance community. This will, in turn, make it easier for the insurance agent/broker to do business in the future. The insurance agent/broker will not be met by an air of scepticism to whatever he says. The regulatory authorities will also not necessarily respond to unethical behaviour of insurance agents/brokers, by imposing new rules or tightening current ones. Thus, as a whole, the insurance industry will have more flexibility in doing business.

It is not possible to give a complete list of acts and omissions which will constitute unethical behaviour. Nor will it be wise to attempt to do so. With questions concerning ethics or morals, as soon as any written criteria are made known, there will immediately be “grey areas” or borderline cases, with a temptation to apply legalistic rigidity to an area in which such an approach is just not appropriate. However, it may be helpful to mention some obvious examples of unethical behaviour, with a view to avoiding such lapses. Hence, the following maybe considered unethical.

A client will probably have little or no insurance knowledge, so it will be easy to give assurances, which in fact are untrue. Sometimes, insurance agents/brokers do this, because they themselves do not understand what they are selling. Such cases are a “double” breach, in that they represent an ignorance which the professional should not have, and a dishonesty which an ethical person should not practise.

Sometimes great liberties are taken with the truth, directly or indirectly, in an attempt to belittle others or justify oneself. In a moral sense, another quotation may help: “I do not make myself big by trying to make you look small”.

The insurance agent/broker may well assist the client in completing a proposal form. He must not tell the client what to say, he must not “doctor” the information given in any way, with or without the consent of the proposer. He must get the client to review or go through what he has filled in the proposal form, before requesting the client to sign it. Also, the insurance agent/broker must not sign the proposal form on behalf of the client.

The insurer relies upon the integrity of the information supplied (in some cases exclusively) to underwrite the risk. Any such interference or undue influence upon the information supplied is, in fact, committing fraud.

The insurance agent/broker must be aware of the important implications of Section 23(5) of the Insurance Act 1966 This section provides that: “No Singapore insurer shall, use in the course of carrying on insurance business in Singapore, a form of proposal which does not have prominently displayed therein a warning that if a proposer does not fully and faithfully give the facts as he knows them or ought to know them, he may receive nothing from the policy.”

In the interest of any of his clients, the insurance agent/broker should explain the importance of this warning, which is in the proposal form, to the client who is proposing for the insurance. This also reinforces the principle of utmost good faith and its importance.

It goes without saying that any cheating, stealing or otherwise illegal act on the part of the insurance agent/broker is unacceptable. This may range from failing to pass on premiums, to dishonest involvement and conspiracy with the policyholder to defraud the insurer.

subsections.label: E
subsections.title: Motivated Primarily By Commissions To Be Earned

paragraphs.text: In suggesting an insurance policy or programme, the question of the insurance agent’s/broker’s remuneration must always be subordinate to the needs and wishes of the client.

While marketing is an essential element of the insurance agents/brokers, all advertising and other promotional endeavours must be consistent with the truth and reasonable belief by the insurance agents/brokers making them. Misleading, as well as outright dishonest selling, is certainly unethical.

For instance, in January 2021, there was an ad on Youtube that offered astonishing payouts where policyholders could “claim up to 500 per cent” if diagnosed with over 100 types of illnesses. However, the ad did not disclose the name of the insurer or how the policy works. Hence, potential customers are required to submit their personal details at a website for the sellers to reach out to them. A compliance officer discovered that the ad had grouped features of different products into the same video, thus misleading customers that they are able to enjoy all these benefits in a single policy. A spokesman for the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) said that they were looking into this case as exaggerated advertising is prohibited and reinforced that marketing messages must abide by official guidelines and approved by the senior management of the financial institution.

subsections.label: G
subsections.title: Mixing Clients’ Money With One’s Own

paragraphs.text: This is normally expressly forbidden in an agency contract. However, even if it is not specifically stated in the agency contract, it is not a good practice to do so.

Every insurance agent/broker must be sure to comply with all laws and rules governing the handling of premiums, because any violation is a breach of the insurance agent/broker’s fiduciary duty, even if no harm is done to the existing or prospective client.
Accounting of all clients’ financial transactions involving money transmissions in respect of insurance arrangements must be properly documented and kept at all times by the insurance agent/broker.

Unless the insurance product offered is clearly more suitable for the client’s needs, no attempt should be made by the insurance agent/broker to persuade the client to cancel and replace an existing insurance cover. True and fair competition is perfectly legitimate in a free market, but business growth must not be achieved at the expense of truth and the client’s interests.

The insurance agent/broker has an obligation to disclose all material facts relevant to the client’s decision to purchase. In addition, it is also unethical not to reveal all pertinent information that has a bearing on the placement of an insurance policy to the insurer. The insurance agent/broker must not withhold facts that the insurer needs to know, in order to underwrite prudently. At the time of application, the client must first fully understand the importance of providing accurate information, and the serious consequences of not doing so.

Agents/brokers gather a great deal of personal, corporate and financial information about their clients. Such information may be of a highly sensitive nature. Other than to provide underwriting information to the insurer, agents/brokers should make it a rule never to discuss business or personal matters of a client with anyone, but the client. The trust relationship between an agent/broker and client will be damaged if the agent/broker fails to keep sensitive information confidential. To violate the trust that clients place in them will be a breach of ethics. K. “Padding” The Premium Quoted & Keeping The Difference

“Padding” the premium quoted and keeping the difference means that the agent actually quotes to the policyholder a premium above the premium as set by the agent’s principal (the insurer), and then pockets the difference.

“Padding” is tantamount to cheating and it is a criminal offence. The ARB under the GIA takes a very serious view of this malpractice and will not hesitate to suspend or cancel the registration of any insurance agent/broker who is guilty of such misconduct. Likewise, the MAS will not hesitate to suspend or cancel the licence of any insurance agent/broker being guilty of such misconduct.

An insurance agent must not engage in sub-agency practice. He must not allow anyone (unless he is a Nominee Agent registered with the ARB) to act on his behalf, to solicit any general insurance business, or to carry out any general insurance selling or advisory activity. The practice of using a sub-agent to distribute any part of its business is unacceptable. Such malpractice will constitute a breach of the provisions of the GIARRs and will result in suspension or termination of the registration with the ARB under the GIA.

Using doctors, lawyers, teachers and others as models of what professionals should be, Dr Solomon Huebner, the founder of The American College, in 1915 cited four characteristics of the professionals, being still remain relevant today: “▪The professional is involved in a vocation that is useful and noble enough to inspire love and enthusiasm in the practitioner.
▪ The professional’s vocation requires an expert’s knowledge in its practice.
▪ The professional should abandon the strictly selfish commercial view and ever keep in mind the advantage of the client.
▪ The professional should possess a spirit of loyalty to fellow practitioners, of helpfulness to the common cause they all profess and should not allow any unprofessional acts to bring shame upon the entire profession.”

These are: (a) Possess A Body Of Specialised Knowledge
▪ Any profession that has at its core a body of subject matter which must be sufficiently technical, so that the average layperson cannot readily understand it. (b) Entry Barriers Into The Profession
▪ An individual cannot be a self-declared professional. A profession needs to have, as one of its hallmarks, a barrier to entry. This frequently involves examinations on the technical subject matter through which the prospective professional demonstrates his competence and expertise. (c) Continuing Education
▪ As knowledge keeps evolving, professionals will continue to keep their professional knowledge and skills up to date through continuing education. (d) Governed By A Society’s Code Of Ethics
▪ Professionals belong to professional bodies and are guided by a professional code of ethics.

Being a professional demand increased responsibilities to clients:
▪ responsibility for delivering high quality informed service to clients;
▪ responsibility to be aware of ethical and compliance issues, and to act ethically in compliance; and
▪ fiduciary responsibility.

sections.title: The Singapore General Insurance Code Of Practice

paragraphs.text: The GIA has developed a General Insurance Code of Practice, which came into effect from 1 June 2004. The latest revised version has taken effect from 25 October 2022 (see Appendix 9A).

The aim of the Code of Practice (the “Code”) is to provide clear and consistent standards for the general insurance industry, so that a better and more informed relationship between general insurers and their policyholders can be established, thereby improving policyholders’ confidence and trust in the general insurance industry.

It also seeks to establish transparency in the insurance products, as well as insurance practices, so that policyholders are able to make informed choices when making purchasing decisions. Insurance products and services covered under the Code encompass all general insurance policies issued to an individual.

The Code advises on the dispute resolution mechanisms and procedures in relation to complaints and disputes between insurers and their policyholders. The revised Code applies only to general insurers and does not attempt to set the standards for intermediaries.
All members of the GIA adopt the Code. It operates alongside the various rules and regulations governing the conduct of the general insurance industry.

If insurers fail to meet with any standards under the Code, policyholders may lodge complaints against them in accordance with established procedures. The Code does not provide anyone with the right to take legal action against any insurer.

The Code outlines insurers’ commitments in the areas as described below: (a) Business Practice For example, insurers undertake to act fairly and reasonably when they deal with policyholders. (b) Advertising For example, insurers will withdraw any advertising and promotional materials if they become aware that the information provided is not accurate, not clear, or misleading. (c) Protecting The Policyholder’s Interest An example is advising policyholders to deal with registered agents or brokers. (d) Confidentiality For example, insurers will undertake to safeguard policyholders’ data and comply with the Personal Data Protection Act (2012). (e) Conflicts of Interest For example, in circumstances where avoidance of conflicts of interest may not be practicable, the insurer’s employee and/or intermediary will disclose to the policyholder the conflict of interest arising from the relationship with the insurer, including any material information or facts that may compromise his objectivity, before the insurance is arranged.
For policies which offer a “Free Look” feature, policyholders are given a “Free Look” period of at least 14 business days from the date that they receive the policy document.

Should policyholders decide not to continue with the insurance purchased, they can cancel their covers within this period and get all their money back, if they have not made a claim. Insurance cover would deem not to have attached, and no benefits shall be payable under the policy. An administration charge may be imposed by insurers.
More details concerning these service standards can be found in Appendix 9A.
The Code of Practice for Agents issued by GIA is part of the General Insurance Agents’ Registration Regulations (“GIARR”).

This Code sets out the minimum standards of conduct and the duties required of an Agent in the conduct of an Agent’s general business as an Agent representing a Member or Members (“Members”) of the GIA.

This Code shall be observed and complied with by all Agents in addition to the provisions of GIARR as may be amended by Agents’ Registration Board (“ARB”) from time to time. This Code including subsequent amendments, alterations and additions shall be observed and complied with by all Agents, Nominee Agents and Trade Specific Agents (TSA).

This Code covers: (a) General Sales Principles For example, the Agent is to ensure as far as possible that the policy proposed is suitable to the needs and resources of the prospective policyholder and he is to give advice only on those insurance matters in which the Agent is knowledgeable and shall seek or recommend specialist advice when necessary. (b) Explanation Of The Contract An example is the Agent is to explain all essential provisions of the cover afforded by the policy or policies which he is recommending so as to ensure as far as possible that a prospective policyholder understands what he is or may be buying and to draw attention to all restrictions, exclusions and conditions applicable to the policy. (c) Disclosure Of Underwriting Information An Agent shall, in obtaining underwriting information or the completion of the proposal form or any other material: (i) avoid influencing a prospective policyholder and shall make it clear that all the answers or statements given are the prospective policyholder’s own responsibility; and (ii) ensure that the consequences of non-disclosure and inaccuracies are pointed out to a prospective policyholder. (d) Accounts And Financial Aspects An Agent shall, if authorised to collect moneys in accordance with the terms of his agency appointment: (i) keep a proper account of all financial transactions with a prospective policyholder which involve the transmission of money in respect of insurance policy; (ii) record receipt of all money received in connection with an insurance policy and shall distinguish the premium from any other payment included in the money; and (iii) remit any money so collected in strict conformity with the terms and conditions of the Agent’s agency appointment and the provisions of GIARR. (e) Documentation An Agent shall not withhold from a policyholder any written evidence or documentation relating to the contract of insurance. (f) Claims For example, if a policyholder informs an Agent of an incident which might give rise to a claim, the Agent shall inform the Agent’s Principal concerned without delay and, in any event, within 3 working days and thereafter give prompt advice to the policyholder of the requirements of the Agent’s Principal concerned relating to the claim, including the provision, as soon as possible, of information that may be required to establish the nature and extent of the loss. Information received by an Agent from a policyholder shall be passed to the Agent’s Principal concerned without delay. (g) Rates Fixed By An Agent’s Principal With the exception of Agents with binders, all rates of insurance shall be fixed by an Agent’s Principal. An Agent shall not quote to a policyholder a rate above or below the rates set out by the Agent’s Principal concerned. As discussed in Section 7K of this chapter, “Padding” is tantamount to cheating and it is a criminal offence. (h) Publicity No advertisements and no printed matter concerning an Agent’s Principal, or its products or services shall be published, circularised or distributed by an Agent unless a copy thereof has first been approved by a duly authorised official of the Agent’s Principal concerned. (i) Premium Refund An Agent shall, upon the refund of any premium due under a contract of insurance, promptly remit the full refund to the policyholder without any claims, set-off or deduction whatsoever unless a claim, set-off or deduction has been pre-agreed by the policyholder. (j) Agency No policy shall be sold by an Agent except within the terms of the Agency Agreement entered into between the Agent and the Agent’s Principal concerned. (k) Sub-Agency An Agent shall not enter into any agreement or arrangement whatsoever for the appointment or engagement of any sub-agent. (l) Conflicts Of Interests For example, in circumstances where avoidance of conflicts of interest may not be practicable, the insurer’s Agent, Nominee Agent or TSA should disclose to the customer the conflict of interest arising from the relationship with the insurer, including any material information or facts that may compromise his objectivity, before insurance is arranged. (m) Dispute Resolution, Mediation, Arbitration Or Legal Proceedings An Agent shall not (unless the Agent’s Principal otherwise agrees in writing) institute or defend or take part in any dispute resolution, mediation, arbitration or legal proceedings in connection with any matter relating to the business of the Principal.
A copy of the entire Code Of Practice for Agents can be found at: http://gia.org.sg/pdfs/GIARR/AMF_CodeOfPractice.pdf 13. SINGAPORE INSURANCE BROKERS’ ASSOCIATION CODE OF CONDUCT 2023
The Singapore Insurance Brokers’ Association (“SIBA”) Code of Conduct 2023 sets out the standards of conduct expected of SIBA Members.

The purpose of the Code is to define and communicate the standards of conduct expected of SIBA’s members in their practice. It is hoped that the standards set out in this Code will help to strengthen consumer trust and confidence in the insurance broking industry.

The Code is divided into five sections, as follows: (a) Section 1 - sets out details about the scope, implementation and operation of the Code. This section contains commitments from both SIBA and Members concerning the operation of the code. (b) Section 2 - sets out the standards that Members have agreed to provide to their Customers. (c) Section 3 - sets out a complaints procedure which Members have agreed to be bound by. (d) Section 4 - clarifies the status of the Code. (e) Section 5 – sets out SIBA’s contact details.

The standards set out in Section 2 of the Code are worded to help Members deliver a professional service to their clients. For each standard, there is a corresponding “General Principle” which introduces what the Member should strive to obtain. The standards must be interpreted and applied in accordance with the corresponding General Principle. The standards are to be interpreted and applied with due regard for the spirit and aims of the Code.

The standards are not an exhaustive list of the duties which Members are required to follow.
